core_claim: Gemma 4 12B is an open (Apache-2.0), encoder-free multimodal model that takes text, image, and audio input and runs locally on consumer hardware — but it only outputs text, and its headline 'near-26B' benchmarks are Google's own.

caption:
Google just dropped Gemma 4 12B, and the headline isn't a benchmark. It's where it runs: your laptop.

It's a dense, encoder-free multimodal model. Text, image, and audio go in, and text comes out. "Encoder-free" means the raw pixels and audio project straight into the model instead of through separate vision and audio encoders, so it's leaner, lower-latency, and you can fine-tune the whole thing in one pass. Unsloth puts the 12B at roughly 8GB of VRAM in 4-bit, so it actually runs on consumer hardware, and it's Apache-2.0, with open weights you can tune on your own data.

Two honest caveats. It's input-only: it reads images and audio, but it doesn't generate them, so it's not a FLUX or a voice model. And the "nears our 26B model" line is Google's own benchmark; verify it on your task before you trust it.

The real story isn't the score. It's that capable multimodal AI now fits on a laptop and never has to touch the cloud.

Run it locally with Ollama, LM Studio, llama.cpp, or Unsloth.

Would you run your AI fully local if your data never left the machine?

Follow for AI breakdowns without the hype.
comment_prompt: What would you actually build with a private, offline multimodal model on your own box?
alt_text: Slide 1 (cover): a sleek laptop on a dark desk with a warm glowing orb hovering above the keyboard; bold headline space below.
Slide 2 (context): a glowing cube in the dark with three streams (light, a sound wave, and a sheet of paper) flowing into it.
Slide 3 (risk): a wide funnel swallowing a photo, a sound wave, and a page, with a single thin ribbon of light trickling out the bottom.
Slide 4 (mechanism): mosaic tiles and sound-wave fragments streaming directly into one glowing core with nothing in between.
Slide 5 (failure_point): a single trophy on a tall pedestal under one spotlight while the surrounding ground stays dark.
Slide 6 (defense): a small glowing cube connected to an ordinary laptop by a bright cable of light.
Slide 7 (takeaway): a warm glowing orb cupped in two open hands above a closed laptop.
Slide 8 (cta): an open laptop glowing in a dark room with a warm question-mark of light rising from the screen.

sources.source: Google — "Introducing Gemma 4 12B: a unified, encoder-free multimodal model" (Jun 2026)
sources.supports: Encoder-free unified architecture; vision + audio flow directly into the LLM backbone; first mid-sized Gemma with native audio input; runs locally on ~16GB; Apache-2.0; MTP drafters. The 'benchmarks nearing our 26B model' claim is Google's own.

sources.source: Google Developers Blog — "Gemma 4 12B: The Developer Guide" (Jun 2026)
sources.supports: Dense multimodal model; encoder-free (raw 48x48 patches via single matmul; raw 16kHz audio projected linearly); unified fine-tuning via Hugging Face or Unsloth; OpenAI-compatible local serving via litert-lm serve.
Gemma 4 supports image, text and audio inputs and generates text responses; the 12B Unified variant is encoder-free and supports audio.

sources.source: Unsloth — "Gemma 4 - How to Run Locally" docs
sources.supports: Gemma-4-12B Unified runs on ~8GB RAM/VRAM (4-bit) or ~14GB (8-bit); GGUF available; run via Unsloth Studio, llama.cpp, Ollama, or LM Studio; Apache-2.0.

narration.text: Google just put a multimodal AI on your laptop.
Gemma 4 12B dropped this week. It takes text, images, and audio, it reasons, and it runs offline on about eight gigs of VRAM.
One honest catch: it's input-only. It reads images and audio, but it only writes text. It can't generate pictures or voice.
It's Apache 2.0 and open. Run it with Ollama or Unsloth, fine-tune it on your own data, and nothing leaves your machine.
Would you run your AI fully local if it never touched the cloud?

beats.caption: Google just put a multimodal AI on your laptop.
Gemma 4 12B: text, image, audio in, runs on ~8GB, offline.
Input-only: it reads media, it doesn't generate it.
Apache-2.0, run it local with Ollama or Unsloth.
Run your AI where your data never leaves?
